question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of businesses in the Newry and Armagh constituency that have received support from the Back in Business rate relief scheme, in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the value of unpaid domestic rates, in each of the last five years, broken down by council area.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the value of rates relief provided to (i) domestic; and (ii) non-domestic, properties in each of the last five years, broken down by council area.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the defects and the structural integrity of New Road, Carrickasticken, Forkhill, will be assessed and repairs actioned.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of jobs created by Foreign Direct Investment, in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of cancelled planned elective surgeries in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Housing Executive properties, in Newry and Armagh, that have been vacant for more than 12 months.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of places provided for tractor driving for 13-15 year-olds training courses, in each of the last five years; and (ii) the number of occasions where the spaces for the course have not been filled.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of incidents of benefit fraud identified, in the Newry and Armagh constituency, in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of additional Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools classes established in Newry and Armagh, in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any capital works to take place within the Newry and Armagh Constituency during 2025-2026.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the current number of vacancies within the Southern Division of DFI Roads.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any outstanding repair works following damage caused by Storm Éowyn in the Newry and Armagh Constituency.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of missed appointments in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last ten years.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Newry Community Care and Treatment Centre.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of international media visits facilitated by Tourism NI; and (ii) the number of international media visits, that included time spent in (a) Armagh City; and (b) Banbridge and Craigavon local council area, in each of the last five years.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of physical assaults that have occurred against medical staff in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) hospital; and (ii) medical site.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of individuals to have availed of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme, since its formation, broken down by constituency.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans to extend the gas network into rural areas of Armagh.
To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any departmental initiatives designed to equip the elderly and vulnerable to access essential services digitally.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the progress of a new build for Tandragee Junior High School.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any engagement their Department has had with the UK Government in relation to the escalating cost of fuel.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of cholecystectomy operations carried out in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, in the last 12 months.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the (i) eroded white line markings; (ii) defective cats eyes; (iii) sunken gullies; and (iv) potholes, on the main A27 from Portadown to Tandragee, will be repaired.
answer_text: Any defects including potholes and sunken ironwork will be identified during inspections and those that meet current intervention levels will be programmed for repair in so far as resources permit. I am pleased to advise that work to replace cats eyes and refresh road markings has been ordered and will commence in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will install additional 30mph speed limit signs on Sleepyvalley, Richhill.
answer_text: There is currently a 30mph speed limit sign in place on Sleepy Valley at the Portadown Road junction.  My Department’s current policy is that we do not provide 30mph repeater signs in street lit areas as this would lead to an over proliferation of unnecessary signs.  Concerns regarding driver behaviour or speeding should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail an estimate of any costs associated with the legislative process to date for the Shellfish Gathering (Conservation) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2026.
answer_text: The expenditure incurred in making the Shellfish Gathering (Conservation) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2026 amounted to £518 (excluding VAT).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 40291/22-27, to detail the number of patients waiting more than one year for routine cataract surgery, in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Cataract lists for Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) are held by Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT), with BHSCT providing outreach ophthalmology including cataract services to residents of SHSCT.To facilitate pooled lists which smooth pathways and improve efficiency and productivity across Trust boundaries, the BHSCT-managed list is not routinely disaggregated per Trust.As of 23 March 2026, the combined overall BHSCT surgical waiting list for a cataract procedure is 451 patients. The number includes both urgent and routine and can be broken down thus: Routine patients 416 Urgent patients 36This is a welcome improving picture facilitated by network of three Cataract Day Procedure Centres (DPC), established since 2018/19. These measures have seen regional urgent surgical cataract waiting lists fall from over 3,700 to almost zero.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a 20mph speed limit can be introduced at Mountnorris Primary School, Armagh.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 41241/22-27, to detail the number of internal staff available for pothole repair within the Armagh depot.
answer_text: The Armagh Roads Depot has a structure of 20 Industrial roadworkers, with 12 of these positions currently substantively filled.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the biennial MOT testing proposals.
answer_text: A synopsis of responses from the consultation has been completed and this will be published in due course.My officials are currently finalising a detailed paper for my consideration on the future of MOT testing here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support services available to treat complex post-traumatic stress disorder.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has carried out an assessment, from a road safety perspective, of road surface deterioration.
answer_text: Road Safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users.My Department’s road safety programme and actions are data driven and managed through an evidence-based approach, delivering engineering, enforcement and education initiatives to address issues that will bring the greatest road safety benefit.While I acknowledge that there has been historic underfunding in our infrastructure, and the winter period has taken a severe toll on the road network, there is no evidence basis to determine that the general condition of the road network is a significant causation factor resulting in road accidents. My officials use PSNI collision data and reports from the Department’s Analysis, Statistics & Research Branch to set priorities, target high risk locations, and evaluate outcomes.However, I am keen to improve our infrastructure with the funding available as it delivers real benefits for our citizens, our communities and the economy. My Department continues to work hard to fix defects on the road network using all the resources available and adopts a risk-based approach to inspection, maintenance and intervention to ensure that the most severe defects are addressed in a timely manner, while road condition and safety are kept under continual review. Keeping people safe on our roads is a shared responsibility and my Department continues to work collaboratively with the PSNI, the Road Safety Partnership, local councils and a wide range of stakeholders to promote safer behaviour and improve outcomes. Ultimately, reducing road deaths requires action from all of us, and as a society we must continue to work together to ensure that, whatever the journey, everyone gets there safely and gets home safe.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will introduce a 20mph speed limit at Derryhale Primary School.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North which will bring the total delivered since 2020/21 to 273 schools https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/20mph-speed-limit-schools-tranche-3. This Tranche has predominantly focused on schools in rural areas where the national speed limit would normally apply.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.My Department is also taking forward a review on a number of speed related matters, including 20mph speed limits which is open for consultation. Further consideration on the use of permanent 20mph zones will be taken on completion of the consultation. I would encourage you to contribute to this significant and important piece of work. Details can be found at Speed limit review launched by DfI | Department for Infrastructure.Concerns about driver behaviour, such as speeding, should be directed to the PSNI on their 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the extent of her Department’s role in the on-site delivery of the street enhancement scheme, due to commence in Markethill, County Armagh.
answer_text: I welcome the £2m Environmental Improvement Scheme for Markethill, Rathfriland and Gilford, which will improve streetscape, pedestrian access and public spaces. The scheme is jointly funded by the Department for Communities, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and my Department, and is being managed by Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council.While delivery will be undertaken by the council’s appointed consultants and contractor, my Department is actively engaged in the planning process, with officials attending project board meetings, participating in handover inspections and providing advice and guidance throughout delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to bring forward a financial support package for young farmers in the absence of a Young Farmers Payment for 2026.
answer_text: My officials are developing a new Farming for the Generations Scheme, which will support farm succession and provide financial assistance to farm businesses with young farmers.    This work is being taken forward in partnership with stakeholders, including the YFCU, and is being informed by the evaluation of the Farming for the Generations pilot scheme and the evaluation of the previous Young Farmers Payment and Regional Reserve scheme. Subject to the necessary approvals and legislative processes, I plan to launch the new scheme in November.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will permit the Policing and Community Safety Partnerships Joint Committee to authorise the partnerships to purchase new speed indicator devices in the 2026/27 financial year.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how he is implementing the Armed Forces Covenant.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that veterans and the wider Armed Forces community receive the right support at the right time in the right way to meet their needs.My officials are progressing a dedicated Armed Forces liaison pathway, which will provide a single point of contact offering a free and confidential needs assessment. This pathway will help identify potential eligibility for benefits, support, and services. It will be available to veterans, service personnel transitioning to civilian life, and their families, helping to reduce the risk of any disadvantage and ensuring they can access the right support at the right timeAdditionally, I have brought forward a range of other actions to enhance wider engagement and co-ordination with Veterans and Armed Forces representative organisations to ensure the services we provide can meet their needs.This includes:The appointment of a senior official as the Strategic lead for veterans’ issues within the Department;Active participation in veterans’ roadshows to improve awareness and access to support. In addition, I have instructed officials to work in partnership with the Northern Ireland Veterans’ Commissioner’s Office in bringing forward further opportunities to promote the full range of departmental services, including housing support, benefits advice, debt guidance, employability services, and other assistance.Improving collaboration with both the Northern Ireland Veterans Commissioner and the Office for Veterans’ Affairs through a trusted partnership relationship where representatives will liaise directly with my Senior Officials on cases already in the system across a range of issues including housing, access to benefits and employability.  Exploring the need for a dedicated forum to identify gaps in provision, improve information sharing, and coordinate support more effectively across partners.From a Housing perspective, my Department and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive are cognisant of the Armed Forces Covenant, ensuring that the Armed Forces community are treated fairly and receive the support they need in relation to social housing allocation, homelessness, and disabled facilities grants. The Housing Executive works to assist all applicants through its Housing Options services to find the best possible solution for their housing needs. All circumstances or needs related to their service history, will be taken into consideration during the assessment of their needs, including referral to the Veterans’ Adviceline, which provides additional help beyond housing. Leaving the Armed Forces can create a real risk of homelessness, and the Housing Executive reflects that in its housing assessment process. The Housing Selection Scheme includes 50 points under the “Other Homelessness” category relating to leaving armed forces service accommodation (for those who may not meet all four statutory tests to be classed as a Full Duty Applicant).Taken together, these actions demonstrate my Department’s com

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider assisting the Department for Infrastructure in undertaking drainage remedial works on Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute land on Drumilly Road, Loughgall, to mitigate residential flooding at the location.
answer_text: Previous investigative works indicate that drainage on DAERA land is not the source of residential flooding at this location. However, should it subsequently be determined by the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) that any such flooding is emanating from drainage on DAERA land then the department will consider appropriate steps to mitigate this in conjunction with DfI.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the available pothole repair resource, within the Armagh DFI depot.
answer_text: All roads operational areas within my Department operate a blended approach using internal and external contractors for the delivery of road maintenance repairs.  Exact numbers cannot be quantified as they are subject to daily change and dependent on a number of variables. In delivering on my commitment to do all I can to make our roads better, I have invested over £40m since December to address the immediate impacts of winter damage. This includes £7.85m for a Winter Recovery Roads Fund and a further £2.51m specifically for road maintenance. This funding is supporting an enhanced structural maintenance programme, including largescale patching and resurfacing, targeted at those areas of greatest need across the North. This work is already underway and is allowing an urgent and focused response to the surface defects that are causing the most concern for road users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether financial support will be provided for the completion of an energy assessment for a homeowner under Support for Low Carbon Heat in the Residential Sector.
To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the HMRC requirements for sole traders and landlords to post quarterly tax updates under Making Tax Digital for Income Tax.
answer_text: I understand Making Tax Digital is a HMRC initiative to modernise the tax system and close the tax gap. My department has engaged with HMRC to understand the scale of the potential impact in the North. I understand that 72,000 taxpayers will be affected locally by the new requirements, with 27,500 of those being initially required to join Making Tax Digital by April 2026, with the rest joining in April 2027, when the threshold is lowered.This is a significant number, and with a particular impact on our small business community, which is a higher proportion of businesses here, relative to Britain.  My officials have been liaising with their colleagues in the Department for the Economy. They will also continue to engage with HMRC on this matter, to ensure that any specific concerns are brought to their attention and that HMRC is providing the necessary support to those who require help meeting any new requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting times for cataract surgery for patients in the (i) Southern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Cataract lists for Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) are held by Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT), with BHSCT providing outreach ophthalmology (including cataract) services to residents of SHSCT.Current waiting times on (i) SHSCT cataract surgical waiting lists are two years for routine and one year for urgent cases. Those for (ii) BHSCT are three years for routine and one year for urgent cases.In order to increase productivity, efficiency and activity around low-complexity cataract cases, the majority of cataract operations are carried out within the dedicated Cataract Day Procedure Centre (DPC) network. As such, only complex surgeries are now undertaken within BHSCT, the majority being delivered within the three regional Cataract DPCs, that at SHSCT (South Tyrone Hospital) being one.Those remaining in BHSCT will have more complex surgical, social, and anaesthetic needs, including the need for general anaesthesia.The network of three Cataract Day Procedure Centres was established in 2018/19 to manage lists regionally, to equalise and reduce waits across Northern Ireland and to avoid clinic and theatre short notice cancellations that may happen in larger more acute hospital settings due to emergencies. These measures have seen regional urgent surgical cataract waiting lists fall from over 3,700 in March 2021 to close to zero today.There are currently 33 patients triaged as urgent waiting on complex BHSCT lists over one year. Clinical and administrative teams at BHSCT are establishing if any of these 33 patients could now be safely managed at a DPC and are attempting to bring this number waiting over one year to zero. The SHSCT list has nine patients triaged as urgent waiting more than one year on the cataract surgical waiting list. This should reduce to zero within this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when road surface defects on the Crossnenagh Road, Keady, County Armagh, will be repaired.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my answer to AQW 40145/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) his assessment of the Energy Cloud NI initiative; and (ii) any planned expansion of this scheme.
answer_text: EnergyCloudNI is a collaboration across the energy generation, transmission, network distribution, and supply sector.   Its purpose is to utilise wind energy which would otherwise be unused to provide free hot water to families living in fuel poverty.In its role as the Home Energy Conservation Authority for Northern Ireland, the Housing Executive is working with EnergyCloudNI to enable this.  They are collaborating to develop a fully automated system to exploit the EnergyCloudNI concept to provide free hot water in the future. In March 2025, the Housing Executive included this workstream in the pilot ‘Rural Leading Energy Transition (RULET)’ project.  This work is in partnership with Ulster University under the SPIRE2 Interreg Project which investigates low carbon heating solutions in a retrofit context.  This pilot of up to 20 dwellings in Omagh is being rolled out to assess the customer’s experience of EnergyCloudNI’s opportunity. The EnergyCloudNI team will evaluate the outputs over an 18-month period which will help inform the potential for future scalability.  Further development would be based on proposals from EnergyCloudNI and considered in line with other funding commitments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the objectives of the Warm Healthy Homes Strategy 2026 – 2036.
answer_text: The Warm Healthy Homes Strategy has six objectivesIncrease investment in domestic energy efficiency schemes for low-income, vulnerable householdsRaise housing standards and improve enforcementImplement a new support framework for energy affordabilityEnsure robust protection and redress for heating and energy efficiencyUtilise and build on experience and knowledge of others to increase energy wellbeingEnsure consistent, accessible financial support for low-income, vulnerable households in emergencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any engagement he has had with the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when her Department will (i) make an assessment of road defects; and (ii) begin repairs, at Sleepy Valley, Richhill.
answer_text: An inspection of the Sleepy Valley, Richill is planned in the coming weeks and actionable defects will be instructed for repair in line with my Department’s current Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the February 2026 Northern Ireland Public Service Ombudsman report Investigation of a Complaint against a GP Practice, when he will introduce updated guidance on GP practice patient removals.
answer_text: I welcome the Northern Ireland Public Service Ombudsman’s publication of the Overview Report on the Removal of Patients from their General Practice Service and the consideration given to this important issue.I fully recognise the importance of appropriate access to General Medical Services provided by GP practices under the GMS Contract, and the vital service delivered by GPs across Northern Ireland.I acknowledge NIPSO’s recognition of steps already taken by my Department to address some of the concerns identified in the report. My Department will give due regard to the three recommendations detailed in the NIPSO report as part of its ongoing work to ensure that all patients have access to the General Practice services they need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact that excessive alcohol consumption has on the health service.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail a timeline for remedial action to prevent flooding and permit the Tandragee to Markethill Road to reopen.
answer_text: The heavy rain of the previous week, combined with Storm Chandra, bringing wind and further significant rainfall, caused widespread flooding issues. Alongside our multi-agency partners, officials worked in difficult conditions to respond and minimise disruption. Officials have attended the B2 Markethill Road repeatedly since the flooding first occurred and the road is now open with flood water being pumped away.  Investigations into the cause and necessary repairs required are ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the Chapel Street crossing patrol in Poyntzpass will be installed.
answer_text: Regrettably, when assessed against these competing priorities, the proposal for a crossing on Chapel Street did not score highly enough for inclusion in the 2025/26 works programme. The scheme will, however, remain on our list for consideration for inclusion in a future work programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total cost of vehicle damage claims in the Newry and Armagh local council area, in the last three months.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to roads related public liability claims can be found online at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20202021-20242025.Table 7 provides details of Public Liability Claim expenditure for the last five full financial years broken down by Section Office, claim type and expenditure type. Please note Section Offices broadly align with Council areas.  Newry and Armagh fall within two distinct council areas which are covered by four Section Offices, namely Armagh , Banbridge and Craigavon East and West and Newry, Mourne and Down East and West.Claim figures have been extracted from DfI's Claims Management System, whereas the financial data has been extracted from the NICS accounting system, Account NI.Unfortunately, as the NICS Accounting System does not record the cause of incidents that lead to compensation payments, it is not possible to disaggregate the amount of compensation paid out by defect type.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 38444/22-27, to detail (i) the financial impact to her Department of recorded offences involving offenders being assessed as being under the influence of alcohol, over the last five years; and (ii) the proportion of departmental resources this represents.
answer_text: The format in which financial information is recorded does not allow (i) the financial impact to my Department of recording offences involving offenders being assessed as being under the influence of alcohol; or (ii) the proportion of departmental resources this represents, to be readily identified and separately reported on. At present, there is no means to provide either direct or indirect cost estimates on the financial impact of recorded offences involving offenders being assessed as being under the influence of alcohol. The Department does not hold information which would afford the ability to disaggregate spend in this particular case. Whilst funding can often be broken down into broad and or specific interventions, the ability to disaggregate the budget spend in further detail is only available under activity-based costing/budgeting or zero-based budgeting, approaches to budgeting which are not currently taken forward across the NICS.  My Department is committed to reducing offending and reoffending by tackling all those factors which influence offending behaviour and as such spend right across the Department can make a contribution to this aim.I can however provide details of my Department’s expenditure on all legal aid cases involving driving-related offences where the offence category indicates that the assisted person had taken excess alcohol. For these identified cases, I have provided the associated legal aid expenditure information, during the last five financial years, in the table below: Financial YearLegal Aid Expenditure£k2020-214002021-226772022-235822023-246212024-25779In addition, Probation Board Northern Ireland (PBNI) have advised that alcohol misuse is a significant criminogenic factor across the PBNI caseload. Costs relating to the PBNI Substance Misuse Court and the provision of PBNI counselling services can be identified and are outlined in the table below:Financial YearPBNI Expenditure£k2020-212992021-222752022-233482023-244112024-25403

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will commit to extending the rating exclusion categories applicable to the rating of empty homes.
answer_text: There are already a large number of excluded categories from the charge for vacant domestic properties. Those categories were carefully developed through detailed public consultation processes, and alignment with similar exclusions that applied within established vacant property taxes in other jurisdictions, so to guard against avoidance.The wider exclusion policy will, however, be assessed by my Department as part of the Strategic Review taking place of all rate supports between now and the end of the 2027/28 rating year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the extent of her Department's efforts to provide salt piles on minor roads, in the Newry and Armagh constituency.
answer_text: My Department commits significant resources to maintaining approximately 5,850 salt bins and 52,300 grit piles across the North that are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met.My Department does not manage or hold information on a constituency area basis however, across Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon West, and Newry Mourne and Down West offices which include the Newry and Armagh constituency, my Department provides 710 salt bins and 10.074 grit piles.  Placement of grit piles at locations which meet the required criteria set out in the Winter Service policy, was carried out ahead of the winter period, taking several weeks to complete. A significant replenishment exercise commenced during the recent cold spell to target those locations most in need, however during such conditions, salt bins and grit piles can be consumed within a few hours.Given the limited resources available to the Department, it can then take some time to again replenish all boxes and piles, at a time when priority must be given to treating the main road network.  Officials endeavour to replenish salt bins and grit piles as soon as possible within the available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of recorded offences, that involved an offender who was assessed as being under the influence of alcohol, in each of the last five years, broken down by offence type.
answer_text: The Police Service of Northern Ireland publish statistics on alcohol-related crimes as part of their Annual Trends in Police recorded crime bulletin.  Under the definition used in that bulletin, alcohol related crimes are flagged as such where it is perceived that the effects of alcohol consumption on the offender or victim was an aggravating factor.  Based on this definition, figures recorded for alcohol-related crimes for the last five years by offence type are provided in the following table.Alcohol-related crime by offence type, 2021/21 to 2024/25Offence Type2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25Violence against the person11,79316,56617,14915,54713,986Sexual offences477766834726735Robbery/Theft/Burglary1,3621,6431,9561,9841,809Criminal damage2,3772,7592,7192,3882,183Drug offences478488436462430Possession of weapons offences266252281317291Public order offences147147141128113Miscellaneous crimes against society377382389370323Total offences17,27723,00323,90521,92219,870Source: PSNINote:The alcohol related crime definition is applied to those offences where it is perceived by the victim or any other person that the effects of alcohol consumption on the offender or victim was an aggravating factor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any support services her Department provides to victims of offences against women and girls, in the Newry and Armagh constituency.
answer_text: I recognise the unique challenges facing all victims of crime, including victims of offences against women and girls. I recognise that it is important that victims are provided with appropriate and timely information, advice and support to help minimise these impacts. The support and information services available are accessible to all victims in Northern Ireland.  The Victim Charter sets out the entitlements and services that victims of crime in Northern Ireland can expect to receive from criminal justice organisations. This includes their right to receive updates on their case. The Charter also provides information about available support services, and what to do should anyone feel their Charter rights are not being upheld. The planned Victims and Witnesses of Crime Bill will include a provision requiring criminal justice organisations to demonstrate compliance with Victim Charter entitlements, which I am confident will help to drive transparency and best practice across the criminal justice system.In addition, my Department funds specialist advocacy services for high-risk / high harm victims and survivors of domestic and sexual abuse, regardless of gender, background or location, which are delivered by ASSIST NI. This service helps victims to engage with statutory and voluntary services and in doing so, helps to ensure victims receive regular updates on the status of their case. Victims are assigned a dedicated support worker who acts as a single, trusted point of contact. Whilst they do not have premises in the Newry and Armagh constituency, their services are available to all within Northern Ireland.My Department provides around £2 million in core grant funding to Victim Support NI to provide a range of support services to all victims and witnesses of crime, including victims of offences against women and girls, and in all Northern Ireland constituencies, including Newry and Armagh. These services include the provision of emotional support, advocacy, information, and advice to victims; assistance with claiming Criminal Injuries Compensation; and court support to adult prosecution witnesses called to give evidence at court.  Together, these services help those affected by crime to deal with the impact, claim compensation and give their best evidence at court.My Department also funds the NSPCC’s Young Witness Service, which provides support and reassurance to young people, children and their families which are available in all constituencies.The Sexual Offences Legal Adviser (SOLA) and Children’s Sexual Offences Legal Adviser (CSOLA) services are available free of charge to all victims of serious sexual offences. The qualified legal advisers can provide independent, general and specific legal advice on the criminal justice process to the victim, helping them to feel involved in and make decisions about their case and supporting them to protect their rights, particularly in relation to personal records held by third parties. Their services are

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the Mullaghbrack Road junction with the main A28 Newry to Armagh road, will be repaired.
answer_text: The resurfacing of the Mullaghbrack Road junction will be kept under consideration for inclusion on future work programmes subject to resources and competing priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the junction with Mullaghbrack Road and Main Street, Hamiltonsbawn, will be repaired.
answer_text: Details of the planned programme for the 2025/26 financial year are included in the Department’s Annual Report to Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.
